fact: January 19 is exactly 29 days before the shortest month of the year ends!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many squirrels typically have extra fluffy winter coats that make them look like tiny gray puffballs with tails.
The sun rises about 1 minute earlier each day around January 19 in most places, which means winter is slowly losing its grip on daylight.
January 19 falls in the middle of Aquarius season in the zodiac calendar.
In Antarctica, January 19 is peak summer, and penguin chicks are typically learning to swim in water that's still freezing cold.
Tree sap in cold regions moves very slowly on January 19, flowing like thick honey through tiny tubes inside the trees.
In places like northern Canada and Alaska, the aurora borealis often appears most vibrant around this time because the long winter nights provide perfect darkness for the dancing lights.
Many houseplants go into slow-growth mode around January 19, growing so slowly you'd need serious patience to notice any change.
January 19 is when hibernating bears in northern climates are typically in their deepest sleep phase, with heart rates so low they're like living teddy bears in slow motion.
The moon often looks extra bright and crisp on clear January 19 nights because cold winter air has less moisture to blur its edges.
In the Southern Hemisphere, January 19 is usually when many flowers are blooming intensely during their peak summer season.
